summary:
The Cardial Pottery culture (also called Cardium pottery or Impressed Ware culture in its broader form) was the first Neolithic culture to spread farming along the Mediterranean coastlines of Europe, dated from approximately 6400 to 5500 BCE. Named after the use of the edge of the cockle shell (Cardium edule) to impress decorative patterns into wet clay, Cardial pottery is found across a vast area stretching from northern Italy and southern France westward through Spain, Portugal, and around to coastal North Africa — covering much of the Mediterranean littoral of western Europe.

The Cardial Neolithic represents one of the two primary routes through which farming reached western Europe, complementing the overland Danubian route through the Balkans. Cardial farming communities appear to have expanded rapidly westward along Mediterranean coastlines by sea, establishing settlements on favorable coastal and island locations with access to marine resources. This maritime Neolithic colonization could have moved with remarkable speed along the coast, potentially covering thousands of kilometers within a few centuries.

Cardial communities brought a complete farming package including emmer wheat, einkorn, lentils, and flax alongside domesticated sheep, goats, cattle, and pigs. The herding of sheep and goats was particularly prominent in Cardial communities, reflecting the suitability of these animals for the semi-arid Mediterranean environment. Settlements ranged from small hamlets to moderately sized villages, often located on promontories or near water sources. The culture shows considerable regional variation across its wide distribution, with local adaptations to diverse Mediterranean environments.

The genetic contribution of Cardial farming communities is clearly detectable in modern southern European populations, particularly in Iberia and Sardinia. Their arrival fundamentally changed the human landscape of the western Mediterranean, transforming it from one occupied by sparse hunter-gatherer populations into a world of farming villages that would form the foundation for Bronze Age and Iron Age cultures of the region.
